guest_info.name: Irving Finkel
guest_info.title: Scholar of ancient languages, curator at the British Museum
expertise: Mesopotamia magic, medicine, literature, and culture

summary: Irving Finkel, a world expert on cuneiform, discusses the origins and decipherment of writing, particularly cuneiform, in ancient Mesopotamia. He shares controversial theories about the early existence of writing and the nature of flood myths, including his work on the Ark Tablet. The conversation also delves into ancient games, the British Museum's mission, and Finkel's philosophical views on language, religion, and human civilization.

main_entities.name: Cuneiform script
main_entities.context: The wedge-shaped writing system that originated in Mesopotamia, lasting for 3-4 millennia, and Finkel's primary area of expertise.

main_entities.name: Sumerian language
main_entities.context: One of the native tongues of ancient Iraq, recorded in cuneiform, and notably an isolate language unrelated to any other known language.

main_entities.name: Akkadian/Babylonian language
main_entities.context: The other main Semitic language of ancient Iraq, also recorded in cuneiform, related to Hebrew and Arabic.

main_entities.name: British Museum
main_entities.context: Where Irving Finkel has been a curator for over 45 years, housing a vast collection of cuneiform tablets and representing the achievements of mankind.

main_entities.name: Göbekli Tepe
main_entities.context: An ancient archaeological site in Turkey (9000 BC) where Finkel controversially suggests evidence of an early writing system exists.

main_entities.name: Ark Tablet
main_entities.context: A Babylonian clay tablet (1700 BC) deciphered by Finkel, containing a flood narrative with a round boat, predating the biblical Noah story.

main_entities.name: Epic of Gilgamesh
main_entities.context: One of the earliest works of literature, written in cuneiform, which includes a flood narrative similar to the biblical account.

main_entities.name: Royal Game of Ur
main_entities.context: An ancient board game with 20 squares, popular across the Middle East for nearly 3,000 years, whose rules Finkel deciphered.

main_entities.name: Lexicography
main_entities.context: The invention of systematic dictionaries and sign lists by ancient Mesopotamians to standardize and teach cuneiform.

main_entities.name: Flood myths
main_entities.context: Narratives of a great flood found in various cultures, discussed in the context of Mesopotamian and biblical accounts.

main_entities.name: Rosetta Stone
main_entities.context: Mentioned as a comparable trilingual inscription that aided the decipherment of hieroglyphic writing, analogous to the Bisutun inscription for cuneiform.

main_entities.name: Chicago Assyrian Dictionary
main_entities.context: A monumental dictionary of the Babylonian language, praised by Finkel as a magnificent achievement in translation and understanding.

main_entities.name: Monotheistic religions
main_entities.context: Finkel's controversial view that their creation brought 'evil' into the world due to dogmatism and claims of sole truth.

main_entities.name: Oral literature
main_entities.context: The tradition of storytelling before written forms, which Finkel believes influenced the structure and content of early written narratives like Gilgamesh.

key_details.fact: Writing originated around 3500 BC in Mesopotamia, between the Euphrates and Tigris Rivers, using clay as a writing support.
The crucial invention in writing was the idea of representing sounds with pictures, which allowed for the recording of language and grammar.
Cuneiform, a wedge-shaped syllabic writing system, lasted for well over three millennia and was used for diverse genres from daily documents to literature.
Irving Finkel controversially suggests that pictographic writing might have been a late development from a much older system of communication, rather than the initial stage of phonetic writing.
Finkel proposes that a carved stone seal found at Göbekli Tepe (dating to 9000 BC) indicates the existence of a writing system thousands of years earlier than traditionally believed.
The decipherment of cuneiform was significantly aided by trilingual inscriptions like the Bisutun inscription, which provided parallel texts in Old Persian, Elamite, and Babylonian.
Sumerian is an isolate language, unrelated to any other known language family, suggesting that related languages may be lost to time.
The Ark Tablet, dating to 1700 BC, describes a round boat (coracle) and a flood narrative that predates the biblical Noah story by at least a thousand years, indicating literary dependence.
Finkel believes monotheistic religions introduced 'evil' into the world by fostering dogmatism and conflict, unlike polytheistic systems which were more tolerant.
The rules for the Royal Game of Ur were deciphered by Finkel from a late Babylonian tablet, revealing it to be a race game combining chance and strategy.

topics: Origins of human language and writing
Ancient flood myths and literature (Epic of Gilgamesh, Ark Tablet)
Ancient board games (Royal Game of Ur)
The British Museum and its mission
Religion and philosophy in ancient and modern societies
Evolution of human civilization and intellect

other_persons_mentioned.name: George Smith
other_persons_mentioned.context: Assyriologist at the British Museum who discovered the Gilgamesh flood narrative in 1872.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Henry Rawlinson
other_persons_mentioned.context: British officer who claimed credit for deciphering cuneiform from the Bisutun inscription, though Finkel disputes his primary role.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Edward Hincks
other_persons_mentioned.context: Irish clergyman and 'card-carrying genius' whom Finkel credits with truly cracking the cuneiform system.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Champollion
other_persons_mentioned.context: Credited with deciphering hieroglyphic writing, mentioned in comparison to Hincks' work.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ashurbanipal
other_persons_mentioned.context: Assyrian king who amassed a fantastic library at Nineveh, which was later excavated.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Layard
other_persons_mentioned.context: One of the excavators who dug up Ashurbanipal's library in the 19th century.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Alexander (the Great)
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned in the context of scholars in Babylon and as a historical figure whose stories lived on after his death.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ludwig Wittgenstein
other_persons_mentioned.context: Philosopher quoted by Lex Fridman regarding the limits of language defining the limits of one's world.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Gardiner
other_persons_mentioned.context: Egyptologist whose grammar book Finkel read before studying cuneiform.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Rundle Clark
other_persons_mentioned.context: Finkel's Egyptologist professor at the University of Birmingham who died shortly after Finkel started university.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Professor Lambert
other_persons_mentioned.context: Finkel's Assyriology professor at the University of Birmingham, described as a 'Sherlock Holmes kind of figure' and 'terrifying'.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Darius
other_persons_mentioned.context: Persian king whose trilingual Bisutun inscription was key to deciphering cuneiform.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Utnapishtim
other_persons_mentioned.context: The hero of the flood story in the Epic of Gilgamesh, who built a boat to survive the deluge.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Noah
other_persons_mentioned.context: The biblical figure who built an ark to survive the flood, compared to Mesopotamian flood heroes.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Graham Hancock
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mentioned for his Younger Dryas hypothesis about a global flood event, which Finkel dismisses as negligible.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Joanna
other_persons_mentioned.context: Irving Finkel's wife, mentioned accompanying him to Kolkata and on the maiden voyage of the Ark replica.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Tom Lehrer
other_persons_mentioned.context: Mathematician and satirical songwriter, admired by Finkel for his wit and music.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Dr. Wernher von Braun
other_persons_mentioned.context: German-American aerospace engineer, mentioned in the context of a Tom Lehrer song about rockets.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Marduk
other_persons_mentioned.context: A Babylonian god, protagonist in a street drama described by Finkel, having an affair.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Sarpanitum
other_persons_mentioned.context: Marduk's wife in the Babylonian street drama, depicted as jealous.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Anu
other_persons_mentioned.context: One of the three top gods in the Mesopotamian pantheon.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Enlil
other_persons_mentioned.context: One of the three top gods in the Mesopotamian pantheon.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ea
other_persons_mentioned.context: One of the three top gods in the Mesopotamian pantheon, who warned Atra-Hasis about the flood.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Atra-Hasis
other_persons_mentioned.context: The human hero of an early Mesopotamian flood narrative (from the Ark Tablet) who was warned by a god to build a boat.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Sir Leonard Woolley
other_persons_mentioned.context: Archaeologist who excavated the Royal Game of Ur boards in the graves of Sumerian rulers in the 1920s.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Tutankhamun
other_persons_mentioned.context: Egyptian pharaoh whose tomb contained boards for the Royal Game of Ur.
